$description: PyTorch & CUDA performance dark-corner benchmarks on Colab T4 — catalog + status tracker. See docs/reference/pytorch-cuda-dark-corners.md for full analysis.

experiments.title: GPU→CPU transfer asymmetry — pinned vs pageable
experiments.description: Benchmark .to('cpu') vs .to('cpu', non_blocking=True) vs pinned copy_() across 1MB–2GB tensors. Root cause: DMA can't target pageable memory; pinned memory gives 9× speedup.
experiments.notes: Also discovered that .to('cpu', non_blocking=True) hits 13.3 GB/s without explicit pinned memory on CUDA 12.8 — undocumented fast path.

experiments.title: .to(device, dtype) combined vs two-step ordering trap
experiments.description: Calling tensor.to(device='cuda', dtype=float32) on uint8 casts on CPU first (4× data size), then transfers. Two-step (transfer uint8 first, cast on GPU) is 3-8× faster.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 3-8× speedup for two-step on large images
experiments.notes: Affects every image pipeline that loads uint8 from disk. Compare for 256×256, 512×512, 1024×1024 sizes.

experiments.title: Ad-hoc .pin_memory() before .to() is counterproductive
experiments.description: tensor.pin_memory().to('cuda', non_blocking=True) is 1.5-2× slower than tensor.to('cuda') because CUDA already creates a pinned staging buffer internally — double pinning overhead.
experiments.notes: Pin in DataLoader, not ad-hoc. Compare varying tensor sizes.

experiments.title: GPU-vs-CPU crossover point — matmul & element-wise size sweep
experiments.description: GPU is NOT always faster. Find the exact crossover where GPU starts beating CPU for matmul (expected ~100×100) and element-wise ops (expected ~10K elements) on T4.
experiments.expected_magnitude: CPU 2-100× faster below crossover; GPU wins above ~100×100 matmul, ~10K elem elements
experiments.notes: T4-specific crossover is unpublished. Most valuable single result for practitioners deciding CPU vs GPU.

experiments.title: CUDA first-call tax — 1.6s hidden initialization
experiments.description: First CUDA call takes ~1.6s (context creation, PTX compilation, cuBLAS lazy init). Subsequent calls take ~30µs. torch.cuda.init() does NOT fully initialize CUDA.
experiments.notes: Measure what torch.cuda.init() covers vs first torch.tensor().cuda().

experiments.title: .item() hidden sync tax — per-metric vs batched
experiments.description: Every .item() call is secretly cudaStreamSynchronize(). Batched .tolist() reduces N syncs to 1. At 50K steps with 4 metrics, per-metric .item() adds ~27% to training time.
experiments.notes: Simulate a training loop with 1K steps. Vary metrics count (1, 4, 10). Use torch.cuda.set_sync_debug_mode('warn') to surface hidden syncs.

experiments.title: torch.where vs boolean masking — hidden sync from dynamic shapes
experiments.description: Boolean masking forces CPU sync to determine output size. torch.where produces fixed-size output — no sync. torch.where can be 2-5× faster for sparse masks.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 2-5× faster with torch.where for sparse masks
experiments.notes: Vary mask sparsity: 1%, 10%, 50%, 90%. Test on tensors 1K–1M elements.

experiments.title: CUDA timing without synchronize() is 10-100× wrong
experiments.description: Without torch.cuda.synchronize(), time.perf_counter() measures CPU submission latency (~5µs), not GPU execution time (~500µs). PyTorch's own benchmark framework had this bug until 2026.
experiments.notes: Compare time.perf_counter() vs torch.cuda.Event for matmul of varying sizes.

experiments.title: Implicit .contiguous() copies — chained ops on transposed tensors
experiments.description: After .T/.permute(), many ops silently call .contiguous() triggering full data copies. A chain of 10 ops on a non-contiguous tensor can copy 5-15 times without the user knowing.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 2-10× slowdown; 5-15 implicit copies in a typical chain
experiments.notes: Use torch.autograd.profiler to count aten::copy_ calls. Chain: matmul + layer_norm + add + relu + matmul × 3.

experiments.title: cross_entropy vs log_softmax+gather — 29× layout trap for LLM logits
experiments.description: F.cross_entropy expects channels in dim=1; LLM logits have vocab in dim=-1. The internal permute creates non-contiguous tensor → reduction falls back to slow kernel. log_softmax + gather avoids the permute.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 10-30× slowdown for cross_entropy with mismatched dim layout
experiments.notes: Use LLM-typical shapes: batch=2, seq_len=512, vocab=50257. T4 ratio will differ from A100.

experiments.title: index_select vs regular indexing — dedicated function slower for 2D+
experiments.description: torch.index_select has optimized fast path only for 1D. For 2D+, x[:, idx] is 2-6× faster because it broadcasts indices and uses pointwise ops instead of a specialized gather kernel.

experiments.title: Allocator fragmentation — allocation order causes OOM
experiments.description: Allocating small-then-large can waste 2× reserved memory because small segments can't merge. expandable_segments:True eliminates this. Same total bytes, different allocation order → OOM or not.
experiments.expected_magnitude: Up to 2× memory waste from bad order
experiments.notes: Compare small→large vs large→small allocation order. Track memory_allocated() vs memory_reserved(). Test with and without PYTORCH_CUDA_ALLOC_CONF=expandable_segments:True.

experiments.title: FP16 eps=1e-8 rounds to zero — silent NaN generator
experiments.description: FP16 can't represent values < ~6e-5. Adam's default eps=1e-8 rounds to zero, producing NaN gradients within 50-200 steps. Fix: eps=1e-4 or higher.
experiments.expected_magnitude: Training diverges within 50-200 steps with FP32-default eps
experiments.notes: Train small MLP with AMP. Compare eps=1e-8 vs 1e-4. Track loss and grad norm.

experiments.title: Tensor Core utilization — AMP vs FP32 matmul size sweep
experiments.description: T4 Tensor Cores: 65 TFLOPS FP16 theoretical vs 8.1 FP32 (8× cliff). Real models achieve only 3-4×. Find the matmul sizes where Tensor Cores kick in and where utilization plateaus.
experiments.notes: Sweep matmul sizes 256→8192. Compare FP32 vs FP16 vs AMP. Compute effective TFLOPS.

experiments.title: torch.compile pipeline analysis on T4 — when it helps vs hurts
experiments.description: On T4 (SM 7.5), inductor can be 21% slower than eager for small models. Triton targets SM 8.0+. cuDNN hand-tuned heuristics beat generic Triton kernels on Turing. Find the crossover where compile becomes beneficial.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 0.8-2× (slower to faster depending on model size/architecture)
experiments.notes: Already benchmarked for 1.2M-param CNN. Extend to transformer encoder block (512 d_model, 8 heads) to show inductor winning.

experiments.title: Non-contiguous max() under torch.compile — 8× layout sensitivity
experiments.description: torch.max(x) when x is non-contiguous triggers 3-stage reduction instead of 2-stage under inductor. Same values, 8× slower. A .contiguous() call before .max() fixes it.
experiments.expected_magnitude: 2-8× slowdown for max() on non-contiguous under compile
experiments.notes: Compare torch.max(x) vs torch.max(x.contiguous()) under compile for transposed tensors.

p1_quick_win.description: High impact, <5 min on T4, <30 lines code — run first
